FITNESS · PRICING · MAY 2026

Gym membership pricing strategy.

Tier design, anchor pricing, founding-member rates, promo cadence, ARPU math, and when to raise prices. The pricing decisions that compound into 30-50% revenue differences between gyms with identical traffic — and the pricing mistakes that quietly cap your operating margin.

The frame

Pricing is a positioning decision, not a math problem

Most first-time gym owners price by spreadsheet — fixed costs ÷ projected membership count + margin. That's how you get to a defensible number, not how you get to the right number.

The right price is the one members in your market are willing to pay for the experience you deliver, anchored against what the alternatives charge. Underpricing signals low quality and traps you in a long-term ARPU hole. Overpricing without delivering value is short-lived. The sweet spot is "10-20% above the most similar competitor" if your brand and experience deliver.

Tier design

Build three tiers, anchor at the top

The most common pricing mistake at independent boutiques is a single price. One-price simplicity feels honest but leaves money on the table — every member is forced into the same offer regardless of their willingness to pay.

The three-tier structure

  • Top tier (anchor): Unlimited classes + perks + something exclusive. Priced 30-50% higher than the middle tier. Expected to capture 15-25% of new members. This tier mostly exists to make the middle tier look reasonable.
  • Middle tier (target): Where you want 50-70% of new members. Substantively the "real" offer — full access at a fair price.
  • Entry tier (catch): Limited access (8 classes/month, off-peak, class-pack). Captures the budget-sensitive prospect who would otherwise walk. 15-25% of new members.

2026 boutique pricing benchmarks

FormatEntry tierMiddle (target)Top tier (anchor)
Yoga / Pilates studio$25-$35/class$159-$199/mo$249-$329/mo
Boutique HIIT / cycling$28-$38/class$169-$219/mo$269-$349/mo
CrossFit affiliate$25-$35 drop-in$179-$229/mo$249-$299/mo
Personal training studioPkg of 8 / month2x/week PT pkg3x/week + nutrition
Mid-size group fitnessOff-peak $59-$79$89-$129/mo$149-$189/mo
Full-service gym$19-$39/mo$49-$79/mo$99-$149/mo (premium)

Regional adjustment: Tier-1 metros (NYC, SF, Boston, LA) +25-40% above table. Tier-3 markets -15-25%. Adjust upward for premium amenities (recovery, sauna, branded retail, member-only events).

Founding rates

Founding-member pricing — get this right

Founding-member rates are presale's most powerful tool. Get the design wrong and you've created a permanent ARPU drag.

  • Discount depth: 30-40% below list. Less than that doesn't drive urgency; more than that signals weakness.
  • Cap the cohort. "Founding 100" or "Founding 150" — specific number, enforced.
  • Lock for life. The locked-in rate is the founding deal's emotional core. Don't break this promise — operators who later try to "transition" founders to higher rates lose member trust and create churn.
  • Plan ARPU impact: If 150 founders pay $89 and 850 future members pay $129, your blended ARPU is $123. Model the long-term impact before you set the founding rate.

Full presale playbook: /gym-pre-sale-playbook.

Promo cadence

Discount discipline

Operators who promo constantly train their market to wait for the next discount. Operators who never promo leave acquisition revenue on the table. The discipline is somewhere in between.

  • New Year (Jan 1-15): One promo. 15-25% off first three months for new members. Highest-converting moment of the year.
  • Summer (early June): One promo, lighter — 10-15% off first three months, or a free initial assessment with sign-up.
  • Anniversary (around your opening date): Membership upgrade offer, not a discount — "free PT package with annual upgrade."
  • Referral program (always on): One month free for every new member referred. Lowest CAC channel; never turn it off.
  • Avoid: Constant Groupon-style discounting, "ending soon" pressure that never actually ends, stacking promos on top of each other.
ARPU math

What good ARPU looks like

FormatWeak ARPU (<)Target ARPUStrong ARPU (>)
Boutique studio<$90$110-$160>$175
CrossFit affiliate<$110$140-$200>$220
Personal training studio<$280$350-$550>$600
Mid-size group fitness<$65$80-$120>$140
Full-service gym<$30$35-$70>$80

The drivers above target: personal training upsells, retail (apparel, supplements), member-event ticket revenue, referral bonus structures that move premium-tier conversion.

Price increases

When and how to raise prices

  • On new members: Raise the rack rate whenever your utilization or class waitlist demand justifies it — typically every 12-18 months. New members never knew about the old rate; there's no friction.
  • On existing members: No more than once every 24 months. 60+ days advance notice. Clear value story (new equipment, expanded hours, additional staff, new programming).
  • Never on founding members if you promised lifetime lock. The brand-trust cost dwarfs the revenue gain.
  • Honest framing in the notice: "Costs have risen and we're investing in X, Y, Z. Your rate goes from $A to $B starting [date]." Don't dress it up; members appreciate directness.
Common mistakes

Five pricing mistakes that cap revenue

  • 1. One-price simplicity. Leaves the premium-tier revenue uncaptured.
  • 2. Anchoring on competitor's lowest visible price. If you match the Planet Fitness price point, you're invisible to anyone who values experience.
  • 3. Constant discounting. Trains the market to wait. Quarterly promos at most.
  • 4. No tier for high-willingness-to-pay members. They want to spend more; give them somewhere to spend.
  • 5. Skipping price increases for 3+ years. Inflation alone erodes ARPU 9-15%; you owe yourself the catch-up.
